text:
Smart contracts are crucial programs on blockchains, and their
5  immutability post-deployment makes functional correctness vital.
2 Despite progress in code completion models, benchmarks for So-
0  lidity, the primary smart contract language, are lacking. Existing
2 metrics like BLEU do not adequately assess the functional correct-
 
r ness of generated smart contracts. To fill this gap, we introduce
a
SolBench, a benchmark for evaluating the functional correctness
M of Solidity smart contracts generated by code completion models.
 
SolBench includes 4,178 functions from 1,155 Ethereum-deployed
3
  contracts. Testing advanced models revealed challenges in gen-
 
] erating correct code without context, as Solidity functions rely
E on context-defined variables and interfaces. To address this, we
S propose a Retrieval-Augmented Code Repair framework. In this
.  framework, an executor verifies functional correctness, and if nec-
s
c essary, an LLM repairs the code using retrieved snippets informed
[ by executor traces. We conduct a comprehensive evaluation of both
 
  closed-source and open-source LLMs across various model sizes
1
and series to assess their performance in smart contract comple-
v
tion. The results show that code repair and retrieval techniques
8
effectively enhance the correctness of smart contract completion
9
while reducing computational costs.

:
v benchmarking, automatic evaluation of datasets, code completion,
i
smart contract, blockchain, code repair, retrieval-augmented gener-
X ation
r

Smart contracts, which are special programs that run on blockchains,
are crucial for decentralized applications. They automatically en-
force agreements without intermediaries and most of them are
written in Solidity, running on Ethereum blockchain. In contrast to
conventional program development, developing blockchain-based
smart contracts is more challenging due to their immutability; once
deployed, their code cannot be changed, making any errors or vul-
nerabilities permanent. This necessitates a greater focus on func-
tional correctness in smart contract development.
Recent breakthroughs in transformer-based Code Large Lan-
guage Models (LLMs), including Codex [5], CodeGen [32], Star-
Coder [24], and so on (e.g., Guo et al. [17], Roziere et al. [38]) have
spurred interest in leveraging LLMs for enhancing the smart con-
tract development process such as automatic code generation [8, 41].
However, current evaluation of smart contract generation mainly
relies on metrics like BLEU and CrystalBLEU [41], or subjective
hand-grading [8], which could hinder smart contract completion
methods from optimizing towards improving functional correct-
ness, as functional correctness is more effective than BLEU for
Solidity code completion. Figure 1 demonstrates this inconsistency:
a correctly completed function is rated correct by functional cor-
rectness but receives a low BLEU score; Conversely, an incorrect
completion is rated incorrect by functional correctness but gets a
high BLEU score. Moreover, existing benchmarks for code LLMs,
including HumanEval [5], APPS [18], MBPP [3] and DS-1000 [21]
all emphasize the functional correctness of generated code. This
highlights a gap in research for systematically evaluating code
LLMs for functional correctness in smart contract generation.
We present SolBench, a benchmark designed to assess the Solid-
ity coding capabilities of LLMs. SolBench uses Differential Fuzzing [7]
to verify if the completed functions are functionally correct, over-
coming current evaluation limitations. The SolBench benchmark
consists of a total of 4,178 functions distributed across 1,155 con-
tracts. We extensively evaluate various LLMs, including both closed-
sourcemodels such as GPT-4o [36], GPT-4o-mini, Claude-3.5-Haiku,
and Claude-3.5-Sonnect [2], as well as open-source models like
Deepseek-V3 [9], Qwen2.5-Coder-14B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-Coder-
7B-Instruct [19], and Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct [26], covering different
series and sizes.
The experimental results demonstrate the significant challenges
faced by these models, particularly in scenarios lacking sufficient

2.1  Language Models for Code Generation
LLMs. In recent years, the rapid advancement of Transformer-
based models has led to the emergence of numerous code comple-
tion techniques. Closed-source LLMs such as Claude-3.5-Sonnet [2]

Trovato et al.
and GPT-4o [36] have played a pivotal role in this domain. Mean-
while, open-source code LLMs, including the StarCoder series [24,
27], CodeLlama series [38], DeepSeekCoder series [17], Qwen2.5-
Coder [19], and CodeStral [30], have also showcased remarkable
improvements in code generation tasks. These models, which boast
billions of parameters, owe their enhanced performance to being
trained on vast and high-quality code datasets. In our study, we as-
sess the effectiveness of popular and latest models, GPT-4o, Claude-
3.5, Deepseek-V3 [9], Qwen2.5-Coder, and Llama-3.2 [26] in various
configurations.
Code Repair Methods. Code repair is a research field focused on
fixing errors in code and has recently been used as a post-processing
step to enhance automatically synthesized code. Approaches like
Self Edit [48] utilize execution traces for code repair, while Self
Debug [6] evaluates Codex’s code repair capabilities across various
tasks. Additionally, methods such as Self Correction [43] and ILF [4]
train models to perform effective code repairs.
Retrieval-Augmented Code Generation. Retrieval-augmented gen-
eration (RAG) techniques have gained significant attention in recent
years for enhancing the performance of LLMs [14, 22, 44, 51]. In
code generation, RAG has been effectively employed to mitigate
the issue of LLMs producing outputs that appear plausible but are
factually incorrect [25, 40, 47]. In this study, we examine the impact
of integrating various code repair and retrieval methods on the
performance of LLMs in Solidity code completion (see Section 4.3).
2.2  LLM-aided Solidity Smart Contract
Existing Datasets and Benchmarks. Prior evaluations of smart
contract generation mainly rely on lexical similarity metrics like
BLEU and CrystalBLEU [41] or subjective hand-grading [8]. Our
evaluation method is automated functional correctness assessment,
which can more effectively evaluate the model’s Solidity code capa-
bilities. Storhaug et al. [41] introduced a dataset comprising 186,397
Ethereum smart contracts, while Yu et al. [46] further expanded on
this, creating a comprehensive dataset with 286,397 instances. In
comparison, the DISL dataset [31] is even more extensive, aggregat-
ing 514,506 unique Solidity files deployed on the Ethereummainnet.
However, these datasets have only performed the extraction and
deduplication of smart contracts. In order to test functional correct-
ness, we further screened out the testable contracts and validated
the correctness of the functions within the extracted contracts.
In this work, we propose SolBench, a benchmark that focuses on
correctness through automated fuzzing.
Solidity Smart Contract Tasks. In recent years, there has been a
growing focus on LLMs for smart contract generation. Storhaug
et al. [41] were the first to employ a fine-tuned model for smart con-
tracts completing. Dade et al. [8] presented MazzumaGPT. Smart
contract summarization has been advanced by approaches such as
SCCLLM [50], MMTrans [45] and evaluations on real-world con-
tracts [29]. For smart contract security, ACFIX [49], LLM4FUZZ [39]
andGPTScan [42] leveraged LLMs to address the vulnerabilities. Jiang
et al. [20] utilized LLMs to streamline the labor-intensive aspects
of the code-smell-finding pipeline.

metadata.section_title: SolBench: A Dataset for Evaluating the Functional Correctness of Solidity

text:
Functional Correctness of Solidity
In this section, we present SolBench, a benchmark for assessing
the capabilities of Solidity code completion. Our approach empha-
sizes automatically checking the functional correctness of Solidity
functions, which distinguishes it from earlier testing methods that
relied on BLEU, CrystalBLEU scores [41], or manual grading [8] to
evaluate the quality of generated smart contracts.
3.1  Dataset Construction
The construction of SolBench consists of four steps:
Step 1: Collection of Smart Contract Data. We utilized XBLOCK-
ETH [52] dataset to collect smart contracts addresses. Subsequently,
we crawled the source code from these addresses using Etherscan[11],
the largest provider of verified smart contracts. Since smart con-
tracts on the blockchain are continuously being updated, our dataset
is also extensible.
Step 2: Selection of Active and Unique Smart Contracts. Due to
the high rate of duplication, we used the MD5 algorithm [37] to
deduplicate identical smart contract codes, ensuring each collected
smart contract was unique. To focus on smart contracts that are
actively used, we checked their transaction activity on Etherscan,
excluding any smart contracts with fewer than ten transactions.
Given the nature of our code generation task, we only considered
functions that had relevant comments, enabling prompting our
models to generate functions and test their functional correctness.
Step 3: Identification of Smart Contracts Suitable for Testing. Un-
like traditional stateless applications, which do not retain any in-
formation between executions, smart contracts need to maintain a
record of their internal state to execute complex logic and maintain
data integrity across transactions. This persistent state is stored on
the blockchain, enabling the contract to track variables, balances,
and other critical information over time. As shown in Figure 2,
the smart contract requires on-chain storage, the contract owner’s
address, to execute certain functions. Therefore, we further filtered
contracts that require on-chain storage for testing, such as those
with constructors. Finally, we deduplicated function comments and

ode Completion and Repair Conference acronym ’XX, June 03–05, 2018, Woodstock, NY

Uniq Func Sigs  3793
Avg Func Lines  10.34
Avg Func Body Lines  7.78

Avg Context Lines  1529
Avg Context Tokens  13025
Table 1: Statistics of SolBench. “Func Count” and “SC Count”
show total functions and smart contracts. “Uniq Func Sigs”
indicates unique function signatures. “Avg Func Lines” and
“Avg Func Body Lines” provide average line counts for func-
tions and bodies. “Avg Func Body Tokens” shows average
tokens in function bodies. “Avg Context Lines” and “Avg
Context Tokens” represent the average line and token counts
of the context associated with SolBench functions.
signatures to construct a benchmark in which functions can be
sampled and evaluated.
Step 4: Verification of Oracle Solidity Functions for Functional Cor-
rectness. In this step, we need to verify that the Oracle Solidity
functions we have extracted can pass functional correctness valida-
tion. This is because some functions call other functions within the
same contract with the “only owner” modifier, which may require
on-chain storage and cause the validation to fail. Additionally, some
functions include mint operations, which are not allowed in our
validation environment and would also lead to failure. Since we are
using differential evaluation (see Section 3.2), we add a statement
uint256 this_is_a_test_variable; within the Oracle function
body as a verification statement, as can be seen in Figure 3. This
creates a difference between the modified function and the original
Oracle function, enabling the evaluator to identify the functions
and associated variables that need to be verified. We retain the
functions that pass the functional correctness validation, and we
also keep the functions that produce a “Stack too deep” error after
adding the verification statement. This occurs because defining an
additional local variable within the function causes the number of
local variables to exceed the limit during the validation process.
Implementation details. We extracted smart contracts from
Ethereum block numbers 14,500,000 to 17,500,000, using MD5 al-
gorithm to ensure each contract was unique, resulting in 67,918
contracts. After fetching their transaction records from Etherscan,
we filtered out contracts lacking at least one commented function
or having fewer than 10 transactions. These were then deployed to
our private blockchain for on-chain storage testing, leaving us with
51,461 contracts and 326,435 functions to sample from. To avoid
data overlap, we removed duplicates based on code comments and
function signatures. This process narrowed our pool down to 2,106
smart contracts and 6,653 functions.

metadata.section_title: SolBench: A Dataset for Evaluating the Functional Correctness of Solidity

text:
Next, we verified whether each
function could be compiled correctly and deployed normally in our
validation environment. The verification passed for 4,178 functions.
Dataset statistics. As illustrated in Table 1, we have systemati-
cally examined various data characteristics of the SolBench bench-
mark, providing a comprehensive overview. In SolBench, there are
a total of 4,178 functions for test distributed across 1,155 contracts.
After deduplication of all function signatures, there are 3,793 unique.

In this section, we will introduce the tasks and methods used for
benchmarking, including the code completion task, code repair, and
retrieval-augmented code repair framework. The task of Solidity
code completion is challenging for LLMs because it heavily relies
on global variables and external contract interfaces. When this
critical contextual information is missing, models often generate
plausible but incorrect code due to insufficient context. For example,
models might use incorrect variables or call wrong functions, lead-
ing to compilation errors (Figure 4). However, including the entire
contract as context may exceed the model’s maximum supported
context length, and the computational cost of doing so can also be
prohibitively high. As can be seen from Table 1, when the entire
contract is used as context, the average number of tokens reaches
13,025. To address this issue, we introduce a Retrieval-Augmented
Code Repair Framework (Figure 4), which aims to achieve excellent
code completion performance with lower computational overhead.
4.1  Code Completion
The Solidity code completion task is defined as follows: for a func-
tion within a contract, given the function’s comments, and signa-
ture, along with a certain length of context within the contract that
appears before the function as the prompt, the model is tasked with
completing the function.
We evaluated the impact of different context lengths on the per-
formance of Solidity code completion, as this task largely depends
on contextual information within the contract. As shown in Ta-
ble 2, in the column with a context length of 0, the best-performing
model, GPT-4o, achieved a pass rate of only 32.77%. By analyzing
the reasons for GPT-4o’s relatively low pass rate, we found that
most errors stemmed from the model incorrectly using undeclared
identifiers (Figure 5). Therefore, simply providing context before the

with or without retrieved code snippets (retrieval-augmented c
uncompleted function can test the model’s code completion ability
based on the given context. We use the Qwen2.5-Coder Tokenizer
to calculate the number of tokens for the context length.
We have also tried using the location of the uncompleted function
in the contract as the center, concatenating half-length context
before the uncompleted function and half-length context after it to
form the total given length context. However, the model performs
slightly worse in this case, which may be related to the way humans
write code, as there is a tendency to implement called functions
first. Thus, the context before the uncompleted function tends to
contain more useful information.
4.2  Code Repair
Although the accuracy of code completion increases with the length
of the context, excessively long contexts result in significant com-
putational costs and high hardware requirements. Therefore, we
tested various code repair methods on SolBench, aiming to achieve
a higher pass rate with lower computational costs.
Code repair is a prompting strategy widely used in existing work.
As shown in Figure 4, first, the LLM completes the test function
of SolBench based on the context of a certain length. Then, the
executor determines whether the completed function is function-
ally correct. If not, the LLM repairs the initially completed function
based on the feedback.

We tested several common code repair meth-
ods:
• Self Edit [48] directly uses the error information returned by
the executor as context to prompt the model for code repair.
• Self Debug [6] first asks the model to explain each line of the
completed function, then uses the error information returned by
the executor as context to prompt the model for code repair.
• Self Refine [28] does not use the error information returned by
the executor but instead asks the model to reflect on where the

completed function went wrong, and then performs code repair
based on the inferred error.
• Self Repair [34] first asks the model to interpret the meaning of
the error information returned by the executor and then performs
code repair.
4.3  Retrieval-Augmented Code Repair
Code repair methods have limited impact on improving Solidity
code completion performance. The best method, Self Debug, only
shows an average improvement of 4.5% as shown in Table 3. This
is mainly due to errors arising from missing key contextual infor-
mation. Even with adjacent context, the model might miss critical
details, or these details might be outside the provided context. To
tackle this, we introduce the Retrieval-Augmented Code Repair
(RACR) framework. RACR enhances function completion by retriev-
ing relevant information from the broader context of the incomplete
Solidity function. As illustrated in Figure 4, if the executor finds
the completed function incorrect, it returns an error message pin-
pointing the problematic line. This line is used as a query to extract
relevant code snippets from the entire function context, forming
Retrieved Code Snippets. If the error message does not specify the
problematic code line, we use the initially completed function as
the query. We evaluated both sparse and dense retrieval methods,
sparse retrieval techniques include:
• LCS (Longest Common Substring) [23]: This algorithm iden-
tifies the longest common substring between two given strings.
In our approach, we use the undeclared identifier from the er-
ror message returned by the executor as the query, and start
matching from the longest substring of the query. If a substring
matches a certain code line, we return that code line. If the error
message does not specify an identifier, we parse all identifiers
in the error message or the initially completed function using a
Solidity parser as the query..

In this section, we evaluate the code completion capabilities of
several chat or instruction-based large language models using our
proposed SolBench benchmark. Additionally, we evaluate various
code repair and retrieval-augmented code repair methods to com-
pare their performance.
5.1  Solidity Code Completion
We evaluated code generation models that cover different series
and sizes. Among the closed-source models, these include GPT-4o,
GPT-4o-mini, Claude-3.5-Haiku, and Claude-3.5-Sonnect. Among
the open-source models, these include Deepseek-V3, Qwen2.5-
Coder-14B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct, and Llama-3.2-
3B-Instruct. Inference tasks for GPT-4o and GPT-4o-mini were con-
ducted via the OpenAI API [35], for Claude-3.5-Sonnect and Claude-
3.5-Haiku via the Anthropic API [1], and Deepseek-V3 via the
OhMyGPT API [33]. Qwen2.5-Coder-14B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-Coder-
7B-Instruct, and Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct were run on 4 NVIDIA A100
GPUs (each with 80 GB memory).

Trovato et al.
• GPT-4o is a high-intelligence flagship model. It is a multimodal
model that can accept and generate text, audio, and images. Its
pre-training data is up to October 2023. We are using the gpt-4o-
2024-08-06 version.
• GPT-4o-mini is a fast and cost-effective small model designed
for focused tasks. It supports both text and image inputs and gen-
erates text outputs, including structured outputs. It can replicate
the results of larger models like GPT-4o at a lower cost and with
less latency. Its knowledge is updated until October 2023, and we
are using the gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18 version.
• Claude-3.5-Sonnect and Claude-3.5-Haiku both released in
October 2024. The Claude-3.5-Sonnect delivers comprehensive
improvements, particularly in the coding domain, where it has
further solidified its leading position with significant advance-
ments. In contrast, the Claude-3.5-Haiku stands out for its afford-
ability and fast response times, achieving state-of-the-art perfor-
mance at an accessible level. The versions we are currently using
are claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022 and claude-3-5-haiku-20241022.
• DeepSeek-V3 is a powerful Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language
model, released in December 2024, with a total of 671 billion
parameters, out of which 37 billion are activated for each token.
• Qwen2.5-Coder-7/14B-Instruct released in September 2024,
is built upon the Qwen2.5 architecture and further pretrained
on a massive corpus of over 5.5 trillion tokens. It supports long-
context understanding and generation with a context length of
up to 128K tokens and covers 92 programming languages.
• Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct released in September 2024, is a light-
weight model designed specifically for edge andmobile devices. It
has been pre-trained on up to 9 trillion public data source tokens,
supports a maximum context length of 128K tokens, and incor-
porates knowledge distillation from the Llama 3.1 large model to
ensure efficiency while maintaining high accuracy.
The length of the context significantly affects model per-
formance, with models performing better as the given con-
text increases. Larger models also tend to perform better.
However, smallermodels (such as Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct andQwen2.5-
Coder-7B-Instruct) struggle with long-context completion tasks,
leading to an increased performance gap compared to larger models,
which also face higher computational costs. Among closed-source
models, GPT-4o achieves the best result of 93.49% under a context
length of 4k. However, in terms of the average pass rate across
all context lengths, Claude-3.5-Haiku performs the best. Among
open-source models, Deepseek-V3 performs the best, achieving per-
formance comparable to the strongest closed-source model, GPT-4o.
We do not test StarCoder2-15B-Instruct and WizardCoder-15B be-
cause these models were fine-tuned for Python instructions and
performed poorly in our tests.
We have analyzed the top five error distributions for GPT-4o
when the context length is 0 on SolBench (Figure 5). The majority of
the errors are “Undeclared Identifier”, meaning the model’s function
completions include identifiers that are not declared.

The remaining
errors account for a much smaller proportion: “Member” errors oc-
cur when the model generates struct types or other structures with
non-existent member names; “Identifier not unique” errors arise
when there is ambiguity in the definitions of generated identifiers;
“Indexed expression” errors happen when the model incorrectly

5.2  Code Repair

Table 3: Evaluation of Code Repair Methods on SolBench
Using GPT-4o-mini. “No Repair” refers to single generation
without repair. “Avg.” shows the average pass rate across
different context lengths. “Avg. CR” measures average pass
rate for repair methods at specific lengths, and “Increase”
indicates improvement over “No Repair”.
We used GPT-4o-mini as both the code completion model and
the code repair model because it is cost-effective and demonstrates
performance comparable to the state-of-the-art model, GPT-4o, as
shown in Table 2. We tested the extent towhich different code repair
methods improve the pass rate under varying context lengths.
Under varying context lengths, code repairmethods achieve
better performance with lower computational costs, espe-
cially as the context length decreases. The experimental results
(Table 3) show that under different context lengths, the code repair
method can effectively achieve better performance with lower com-
putational costs, and the improvement becomes more significant as
the context length decreases. When the context length is 0, applying
code repair can increase the accuracy by an average of about 6%.
Among all tested context lengths, the Self Debug method performed
the best, achieving an average pass rate increase of about 4.5%. In
contrast, the Self Refine method showed the poorest performance,
with almost no improvement. This is because Self Refine does not

utilize error information provided by the executor but instead re-
lies on the model’s own reflection of errors, lacking real external
feedback. This further highlights the importance of executor feed-
back for accurate code repair. Additionally, the Self Edit and Self
Repair methods, which also use executor feedback, demonstrated
performance very close to that of the Self Debug method.
5.3  Retrieval-Augmented Code Repair
5.3.1  Assessing Retrieval Methods.  In our experiments with a con-
text length of 1k and 256 tokens, we used GPT-4o-mini. The max-
imum number of generated tokens, the line length of the sliding
window, and the sliding step were set to 1024, 1, and 1, respectively.
The maximum number of retrieved code snippets was set to 2.
Introducing the retrievalmechanismsignificantly enhances
the performance of various code repair methods. The im-
provement is more pronounced with a context length of 256
than with 1k. Among these, the LCS method stands out as
the most practical, delivering top results for short contexts
and near-optimal outcomes for longer contexts at minimal
computational cost.

As shown in Table 4, the performance of
various code repair methods improved after introducing the re-
trieval mechanism, indicating that incorporating context (obtained
through retrieval) can effectively enhance the performance of code
repair. It can also be observed that the improvement with a context
length of 256 is much greater than that with a context length of
1k. When the context length is set to 1k tokens, the average im-
provement brought by different retrieval methods was similar. The
best-performing method is UniXCoder, which provided an aver-
age improvement of approximately 3%. Among the sparse retrieval
methods, the best performer was LCS, whose effectiveness was
almost on par with the best retrieval method, UniXCoder, while
requiring fewer computational resources compared to UniXCoder.
When the context length is 256 tokens, the best-performing method
is LCS, and it clearly outperforms other retrieval methods. There-
fore, the LCS method is the most practical, achieving the best results
when the context length is small and almost the best results when
the context length is large, with very low computational costs..

We have introduced SolBench, a pioneering benchmark for assess-
ing the Solidity coding capabilities of LLMs through functional
correctness. Our assessment of models like GPT-4o, Claude-3.5,
Deepseek-V3, Qwen2.5-Coder, and Llama-3.2 highlighted perfor-
mance challenges primarily due to limited context. To address these

SolBench: A Dataset and Benchmark for Evaluating Functional Correctness in Solidity C
issues, we introduced a retrieval-augmented code repair approach,
which significantly enhanced both performance and efficiency. Sol-
Bench’s extensible dataset ensures adaptability to the evolving
landscape of smart contracts, and we plan to keep it updated to
ensure its continued relevance in assessing Solidity coding skills.
